name: Kael Barba
event_rankings: Astronomy, Chem Lab, Materials Science
why_compete: I want to compete in this tournament because Science Olympiad aligns perfectly with my academic interests and the areas I’ve been deeply studying on my own. This would be my first competition, and I’m excited for the challenge, the accountability, and the chance to learn from teammates and other competitors. For Astronomy, I’m motivated by my passion for astrophysics and gravitational-wave research. I’ve spent the past year working on simulations involving black holes and orbital dynamics, and this event gives me the perfect opportunity to deepen my understanding of stellar evolution, exoplanets, and cosmology in a competitive environment. For Materials Science, I’m drawn to how chemistry and physics combine to explain the real-world behavior of materials. I’m especially interested in how microscopic structure affects macroscopic properties, and I want to build a strong foundation that will help me as I expand into engineering projects. For Chem Lab, I want to sharpen my practical chemistry and analytical-thinking skills. I’ve taken AP Chemistry and enjoy the hands-on and conceptual sides of the subject, so this event gives me the chance to apply that knowledge under timed conditions and improve my lab-based reasoning.
preparation_plan: If I'm accepted, I want to make sure I'm truly ready for all three events, so I'll follow a structured prep plan. For Astronomy, I’ll dive into topics like stellar classification, exoplanet detection, and DSOs like supernovae. I’ll use the rules manual and lots of old test questions to guide my studying. For Materials Science, I’ll put together detailed notes on things like polymers, ceramics, phase diagrams, and how different materials behave. For Chem Lab, I’ll brush up on AP-level concepts, practice problems on stoichiometry and equilibrium, and learn all the lab techniques mentioned in the rules. Along the way, I’ll set weekly goals for myself, take practice tests with a timer, and complete assignments to check my progress. I’ll keep my scioly team updated so they can see how I’m improving. I also plan to revisit and revise my notes regularly, focus on areas I find tricky, and simulate real test conditions so I feel confident and prepared when the time comes.
question_3: Even though this is my first tournament, I’ve already started preparing. I’ve been going through the rules for each event, getting my binders organized, and starting to put together detailed notes. I’m watching lectures on specific topics, reading from trusted sources like NASA, university materials science modules, and AP Chemistry resources, and making sure I have all the key formulas and concepts outlined. I’ve also tried a few unofficial practice tests to get a feel for the timing and how tough the questions can be. I will be submitting my work to the Scioly website or to my coaches in the following couple of days. Plus, I’m practicing how to quickly understand graphs, data tables, and lab scenarios. To keep myself on track, I made a weekly study schedule and started doing regular progress check-ins. I’m really committed to putting in the work and building up the skills I’ll need to feel confident on competition day.

name: Jeun Ebat
event_rankings: Astronomy, Experimental Design, Bungee Drop, Remote Sensing, Dynamic Planet
why_compete:
Astronomy- I want to compete in Astronomy because I am very experienced in the topics of Stellar Evolution and Pulsating Variables. Furthermore, my digital binder has an organized structured of information, making it convenient for answering questions. One thing newer members struggle with are the DSO’s and Calculations, both of which I have experience in, therefore, I believe I would be a good candidate for Astronomy.

Remote Sensing - I want to compete for Remote Sensing because satellites and climate change are important events to study. Newer members often struggle with going through physical binders. My strengths in this event is satellite image, data interpretation, significance, and calculations, all of which newer members struggle with. Therefore, I believe I would be a good candidate for Remote Sensing.

Dynamic Planet - I want to compete for DP because I am experienced with the competition format of fhe event. DP is a very information varied and based events, therefore, a good structured organized binder is necessary and finding through information is necessary, which is what a lot of new members struggle with. Therefore, I believe I would be a good candidate for DP.

Bungee Drop - I want to compete for Bungee Drop because its a fun event, but also, it requires a lot of testing and the calculations to set-up. And finding which string length is suitable to drop the minimum height of the object released, which is what a lot of newer members struggle with. Therefore, I believe I would be a good choice for Bungee.

Experimental Design -  (IMPORTANT, PLEASE READ) - Unfortunately, this event is conflicted with Astronomy. However, if Astronomy is unavailable, consider this alternatively - I want to compete for EXPD because I am familiar with the rubric and the scoring breakdown, and what scores full points and what doesn’t. Furthermore, I am experienced with time and speed (with good partners), which is what a lot of good partners struggle with. Therefore, I believe I would be a good candidate for EXPD.
preparation_plan:
Before competition day, I plan to focus on targeted preparation for each of my events.
For Astronomy, I will review stellar evolution, exoplanet detection methods, and run practice problems using past test packets to strengthen my speed and accuracy. Most importantly, I will research DSO’s and calculations, which are important topics.
For Remote Sensing, I’ll refine my understanding of satellite instrumentation and practice interpreting real-world data images, applying previous invitational tests to simulate timed conditions.
For Experimental Design, my partner and I will practice rapid brainstorming under time pressure and run mock designs with statistical analysis to improve organization and scoring efficiency.
For Bungee Drop, I will collect data through repeated test trials and analyze mass–distance relationships using regression to minimize error.
For Dynamic Planet, I will focus on reviewing contest-specific topics (especially cryosphere and ocean circulation concepts) and complete formula-based scenario practice.

Overall, I will commit to regular collaboration with partners, timed mock tests, and data-based experimentation to ensure consistent, high-level performance on competition day.
question_3:
I want to compete in the South Windsor Invitational because, as a member, I want to contribute to the Bronx Science Science Olympiad Team. This year, I am excited and driven by the team's goal of qualifying nationally. With a solid foundation in Science Olympiad and a deep understanding of biology and STEM, I believe I can contribute my best efforts to the team. 

Past influences that made me qualified to compete are this: I got ≤ 5 ranked for most of the events that I took for the diagnostic. Furthermore, I won Intra-Team for 3 of my events (Astro, EXPD, Dynamic), showing that I already have a history of doing well compared to my peers. Furthermore at Lexington, which was my first competition, I was very close to medaling, with only 1-3 places off the rankings. And while I don’t know my results for Mason, I’m sure I did well on my events. As you can see, I am very experienced in competitions, so I know competition format and how to work well with my partners and solo. 

What makes me generally qualified to compete was from the moment I dedicated myself to improving my knowledge and collaborating across classrooms to foster a passion for STEM, even in a challenging environment. From a lot of experience and preparation last year, this year, and more to come, these achievements remain a cherished memory and a source of inspiration that fuels my desire to continue my Science Olympiad journey at Bronx Science. It is an honor for me to be part of this team, and I will be grateful for any decision made by the logistics team. I hope that our Science Olympiad Team becomes like the unstoppable current of the Mississippi River piercing through obstacles. Flying to the moon and beyond!

name: Matthew Huang
event_rankings: Dynamic Planet, Chem Lab, Disease Detectives, Experimental Design, Forensics, Materials Science
why_compete:
Firstly, I would like to compete in the South Windsor Invitational because it will allow me to develop a better understanding of my events. Preparing for this tournament will help me to identify gaps in my knowledge of each event (especially for the more memorization-heavy ones like Dynamic Planet and Disease Detectives) that I can focus on instead of studying content/skills that I am already doing well with consistently. Additionally, through preparation for this tournament I can test out different study strategies to see which is most effective for each event. 
	Secondly, participating in this tournament will give me the opportunity to collaborate with others. Before the tournament, I will have to communicate with my assigned partner for each event and work with them to make sure that we are both fully prepared. During the tournament, good teamwork/coordination will be useful in ensuring our success. In addition, competing in an in-person event like the South Windsor Invitational will allow me to meet with other students from SciOly teams across the northeast.
	Lastly, competing in the South Windsor Invitational will give me necessary experience for higher-stakes competitions like regionals or states. Specifically, I will be able to get more familiar with the pacing of events in a tournament setting.

Dynamic Planet - I’ve had past experience with oceanography through various summer camp programs. This is also the event that I have dedicated the most time to studying outside of meetings (mainly through reviewing/taking notes on slides). 
Chemistry Lab - I took AP Chemistry during sophomore year and am currently enrolled in Introduction to Organic Chemistry, so I already have some experience with kinetics/stoichiometry. 
Disease Detectives - Disease Detectives involves a decent amount of statistical (odds ratio, relative risk, etc.) and graphical (epicurves) analysis, which differs from DP and Chem Lab. 
Experimental Design - I’ve had the opportunity to design experiments during labs for regents bio, AP chem, and AP Physics 1. Additionally, I was a student researcher at Stanley Manne during 9th and 10th grade and am currently in the biology research program. 
Forensics - Forensics requires participants to link pieces of evidence to a suspect. This is similar to Disease Detectives, where data about an outbreak is used to determine a source.
Materials Science - I have some background in chemistry (Chem lab, courses) and physics (AP Physics 1). My exploravision group in junior research is proposing a technology involving metal organic frameworks (relevant to nanomaterials).
preparation_plan:
If accepted, I will first take practice tests to determine gaps in my knowledge for each of my events. Instead of studying the content for each event generally, I will mainly focus on studying the areas identified from the diagnostics and will prioritize those topics for events that allow only a double-sided reference sheet). The main resources that I plan to use include information from the SciOly wiki, modules on the BxSciOly website, and practice problems/tests from the science olympiad website. Although some time reading notes/watching relevant videos might be helpful, I plan to mainly complete practice questions for each subtopic to make sure that I have a conceptual understanding/properly justify my answers. To accomplish this, I will set aside 2 hours per weekday (and possibly more on weekends) for studying. At the end of the first week, I will take another round of timed practice tests. This will not only allow me to see if I have improved, but also enables me to identify new/continued weakspots.

I will also make sure to contact partners for each event as soon as possible and go over our understanding of the topics together. If possible, I will meet with them during and outside of SciOly meetings to study.

Current Things to Work On (based off of the Mason non-competitor practice tests assigned)
Dynamic Planet: tides, plate tectonics, meteorological influences
Chem Lab: redox reactions, net ionic equations
Disease Detectives: symptoms of different diseases, statistics for the 2x2 table, sketching epicurves
question_3:
Dynamic Planet: I’ve dedicated around two hours per week to taking notes on/understanding the concepts mentioned in lessons (information that couldn’t be covered within meeting time) and completing the assigned homework. Additionally, I have reviewed content beyond what we have covered so far in lessons through the DP binder. Since I didn’t compete in the Mason Invitational for DP, I took a practice test. 

Chemistry Lab: I have prepared for this event outside of class time by reviewing kinematics through my notes from AP Chemistry and the resources posted by the EMs. I have also used both the Regents Chemistry and AP Chemistry reference sheets to find relevant equations to kinematics, solubility rules/exceptions, etc. Since I didn’t compete in the Mason Invitational for Chem Lab, I took a practice test. 

Disease Detectives: So far, I have prepared for Disease Detectives by reviewing the history of epidemiology and terms in epidemiology from the SciOly wiki. I’ve also practiced creating case definitions and interpreting epicurves. Since I didn’t compete in the Mason Invitational for Disease Detectives, I took a practice test.

Other Events (ExpD, Forensics, MatSci): I took the diagnostics for all three of these events, so I am already familiar with the rules and topic lists for each one. For each diagnostic, I created reference sheets with information that I was unable to memorize beforehand. Some of these events have overlapping topics/formats with the three that I am enrolled in.

name: Xingtong Chen
event_rankings: Designer Genes, Remote Sensing
why_compete:
I want to compete in this tournament because it is a great opportunity for me to put everything I have learned so far into practice. Competing in this tournament will allow me to solidify my understanding of my events by applying my knowledge in a competition setting. Beyond the academic reasons, competing in this tournament would also be a memorable learning experience that would help me strengthen my collaboration, efficiency, and critical thinking skills. Overall, this tournament is an opportunity for me to challenge myself, learn, and contribute to the team. 

I want to compete in Designer Genes because I am very interested in genetics, and I enjoy learning about topics such as biotechnology, gene regulation, and gene expression. A lot of the content in this event connects directly to what I have learned in AP Biology, which I earned a 5 on. This background makes me more well-prepared and confident in the foundational concepts, allowing me to master the more advanced materials for this event more easily and be better prepared for the tournament.
I want to compete in Remote Sensing because I find Earth Science and satellite imagery very interesting. This event combines technology and environmental science in a unique way that I find very intriguing, and I am challenged through interpreting what images and data represent. Competing in Remote Sensing would help me learn how to apply scientific principles to better understand the planet.
preparation_plan: If accepted, I will allocate 1-2 hours every weekday and 2-3 hours every weekend to prepare for my events. For Designer Genes, I will review key genetic concepts, study biotechnology, practice with molecular genetics, and look over practice exams. For Remote Sensing I will practice interpreting satellite imagery, analyzing environmental patterns, and studying other fundamental concepts of Remote Sensing. I will also practice with practice exams and past exams that are available. I will review the learning modules provided and other resources to make detailed cheat sheets/binders that will ensure that I can perform effectively in the tournament.
question_3: I have been looking at the links under "Resources and Links 2026" on the Science Olympiad website (https://www.soinc.org/) for all my events so far and familiarizing myself with the content that each PDF covers. I have also been looking at practice test questions such as the 2023 MIT Invitational one for Remote Sensing and a sample of tournament questions for Designer Genes. In addition, I have been completing all homework assignments, which helps me gain a better understanding of the content covered in meetings. I have also been reviewing the resources provided in the modules. Beyond that, I have been watching videos about the events and specific topics (such as ones from Sciolympia) to reinforce my knowledge.

name: Lia Skarabot
event_rankings: Circuit Lab, Robot Tour, Materials Science, Electric Vehicle, Chem Lab
why_compete:
I've always loved competing, and I really enjoyed my first competition this year at Masons. During the competition, there were many things that I learned that I would like to apply to my next competition. 
1) Circuit Lab - I have an extensive knowledge on circuits and deeply enjoy studying them. Due to the Masons Invitational, I understand which areas I struggle with and I will continue to study those areas more in preparation for the next competition.
2) Robot Tour - When I was preparing for the Masons Invitational, I realized how much I enjoyed learning how to code the robot. However, there were some techniques that I wasn't able to incorporate into the robot due to the preparation time before the competition, and I would like to see if I can incorporate these techniques for the next competition.
3) Material Science - Material science is one of my passions and during my few weeks in this event, I realized that I genuinely enjoy studying it. I hope to expand my knowledge in this topic when studying for the next competition.
4) Electric Vehicle - I deeply enjoyed learning how to run the vehicle when preparing for Masons, and I would love to try to improve building it so that it runs better.
5) Chemistry Lab - Throughout my time in this event, I realized how much I love studying chemistry, and I would love to expand my knowledge by competing in this event. I also learned a lot about this subject in AP Chemistry and I hope to apply that knowledge here.
preparation_plan: For Robot Tour, I would try to learn how to keep my robot on a straight path by learning how to incorporate encoders or calibrate it for robot tour. For Circuit Lab, I would study some of the subjects that I don't know as well like how to calculate capacitors or operational amplifiers. For Material Science I would look over my past homeworks and make sure that I knew all of the concepts and calculations, and do a lot of practice problems. For Electric Vehicle, I would try to experiment with different motors and see how to make it run fast without deviating from the path as much. For Chemistry Lab, I would look over my AP Chemistry notes and also do a lot of practice problems. For Electric Vehicle I plan on doing a lot of practice runs before the competition, as well incorporating a better design.
question_3: I made a good cheat sheet (which I have updated since the diagnostics) for all of my academic events. I have an extensive knowledge in circuits from my time studying them and I know what specific topics I have to work on for the next competition. I also studied Material Science and Chemistry Lab a lot during my weeks at those events and I feel very comfortable in these events. I also previously took AP Chemistry, and I can apply what I learned there to these events. In addition, I feel more adept in using the Arduino code for Robot Tour and Electric Vehicle because came many times during the week of competition to practice running the robot and I took it home to continue practicing. I know exactly what I should continue to improve in order to compete in the next competition for my build events.
